senses.definition: Toward or relating to time; moving or oriented in the direction of time.
examples: The clock hands pointed timeward as the day began.
A timeward glance implies anticipation of what lies ahead.

senses.definition: Work performed within a set time or according to a time-based schedule.
examples: The project was billed as timework rather than deliverable-based.
Timework is common in consulting contracts.

senses.definition: A person who works according to a time-based schedule; a shift worker.
examples: The factory employed many timeworkers who cycled through shifts.
As a timeworker, she trained to adapt to rotating hours.

senses.definition: Multiple people who work according to time-based schedules.
examples: The hospital hired several timeworkers to cover night shifts.
Timeworkers across departments organized a union.

senses.definition: Projects or tasks completed within a set time; time-based works.
examples: The firm reviewed several timeworks before approving funding.
Timeworks often pressure teams to accelerate development.

senses.definition: Worn or weathered due to age; showing signs of long use.
examples: The timeworn manuscript bore faded ink.
A timeworn smile crossed his face as he remembered the past.

senses.definition: Plural or proper noun form; may refer to a name or fictional term; no common sense in general English.
examples: In the novel, Timias appears as a guardian figure.
Timias is not a standard English word outside proper nouns.

senses.definition: Lacking courage or confidence; easily frightened.
examples: The timid child hid behind her mother.
He was too timid to speak up in the meeting.

senses.definition: More timid; showing greater lack of courage or confidence.
examples: She was timider than before and avoided crowds.
In the darker scenario, he becomes timider.

senses.definition: Most timid; having the least courage or confidence.
examples: She remained the timidest in the group.
Among the applicants, he was the timidest by far.

senses.definition: The quality of being timid; lack of courage or confidence; fears or apprehensions.
examples: Her timilities kept her from trying new things.
Timidities about failure can hold a person back.

senses.definition: The state of being easily frightened or shy; lack of boldness.
examples: Her timidity prevented her from singing in public.
Timidity can stop people from asking questions.

senses.definition: In a shy or hesitant way; with timid behavior.
examples: She spoke timidly, unsure of her answer.
He looked around timidly before entering the room.

senses.definition: The quality of being timid; timidity.
examples: Her timidness made it hard to try new activities.
Overcoming timidness is part of growing up.

senses.definition: Plural of timidness; multiple instances of timidity.
examples: The report notes several timidnesses across departments.
Their timidnesses hindered innovation.

senses.definition: Full of timidity; timid; fearful.
examples: A timidous voice trembled as she spoke.
The timidous cat hid under the sofa.

senses.definition: The choice or arrangement of when something happens; schedule of events.
examples: The timing of the release was perfect.
Poor timing ruined the joke.

senses.definition: The act of measuring or controlling when something happens; the procedure of timing.
examples: The timing of the engine is crucial.
They adjusted the timing to improve performance.

senses.definition: The times at which events occur or are scheduled to occur; schedules.
examples: Flight timings have changed this week.
Meal timings are different on weekends.

senses.definition: Somewhat timid or cautious; not fully brave or bold.
examples: He gave a timish nod before agreeing.
Her timish nature made her hesitate before speaking.

senses.definition: A person who is optimistic; an optimist.
examples: As a timist, she always expects the best outcomes.
The timists in the group tended to cheer everyone up.

senses.definition: Multiple people who are optimistic; a group of optimists.
examples: The conference included several timists who advocated for innovation.
Timists shared hopeful forecasts for the market.

senses.definition: A person who makes or repairs frames or structures, or a term for a carpenter; in some contexts a Dutch surname.
examples: The timmer worked on the wooden beams.
A timmer helped restore the old house.

senses.definition: No widely recognized meaning in modern English; likely a misspelling or nonce form.
examples: Timne does not appear in standard dictionaries.

senses.definition: A shortened or nonce form; may refer to a name or brand; not a standard English word.
examples: Timo could be a nickname or a brand name.

senses.definition: Systems of government ruled by time-based criteria or, more commonly, a misspelling of timocracies referring to rule by property or wealth; not standardly used.
examples: Timocracies as a political concept is rarely discussed in modern texts.
